html:
Lii Hen is not the clean “tariff safe harbor” story in the prior draft. It is a more interesting, messier setup: a crushed Malaysian furniture exporter with North America concentration, a real tariff shock, negative current earnings, and a balance sheet so overcapitalized that the market is valuing the operating business at less than zero.

At about MYR 0.31 per share, Lii Hen’s market cap is roughly MYR 167 million. FY2025 was ugly: revenue fell to MYR 522.5 million from MYR 639.6 million, and the company lost MYR 12.3 million, with Q4 revenue down to MYR 108.3 million and a MYR 5.4 million loss. The latest filings do not support the prior claim of a 2026 recovery quarter yet. The next real proof point is the March 2026 quarter, expected in late May.

The stock is hated for good reasons. Lii Hen derives more than 90% of sales from North America, customers are major US importers and retailers, and US tariffs on covered upholstered wooden products, cabinets and vanities remain a live problem. The White House kept the current 25% tariff in place while delaying the scheduled step-up to 30% and 50% until January 1, 2027. That is not bullish in isolation, but it does change the risk profile: the worst near-term escalation has been deferred, while US buyers still need non-China, auditable, scale furniture suppliers.

The variant perception is that the market is pricing Lii Hen as if the tariff regime permanently destroys the company, while the balance sheet and cycle math say the equity is closer to a paid option on normalization. At FY2025 year-end, Lii Hen had about MYR 215 million of cash and short-term investments, MYR 35.5 million of total debt, and MYR 179.5 million of net cash, or roughly MYR 0.33 per share. Net tangible book value is about MYR 0.96 per share. At MYR 0.31, the stock trades near net cash and about 0.32x tangible book.

That cash is not automatically upside. Management has not been aggressively returning it, the dividend collapsed to 0.5 sen, and the manufacturing business can burn value if tariffs persist. But it matters because this is not a levered melting cube. Lii Hen can survive a bad order cycle, absorb working-capital volatility, keep customers engaged, and wait for either tariff renegotiation, customer price pass-through, or a US housing and furniture replacement recovery.

The upside case does not require a return to the FY2022 boom. In FY2022, revenue was about MYR 731 million and net profit was MYR 74.8 million. In FY2024, before the deeper 2025 tariff damage, the company still earned MYR 17.0 million on MYR 639.6 million of revenue. If Lii Hen can merely recover to MYR 575-600 million of revenue with a mid-single-digit net margin, net profit could return to MYR 25-30 million, or roughly 4.5-5.5 sen per share. On today’s market cap, that is 5.5-7x normalized earnings before giving credit for net cash.

The torque comes from operating leverage. FY2025 gross margin collapsed to 6.4%, versus 14.6% in FY2024 and 21.6% in FY2022. A move back to even 11-13% gross margin would transform the P&L because the fixed manufacturing base is already in place. Lii Hen’s annual report also shows 90% local trade procurement, solar cost initiatives, no substantiated human-rights complaints, and client-audited worker accommodation, which matter because US furniture buyers under tariff and forced-labor scrutiny cannot simply swap into any low-cost supplier.

The stock is also ignored in the right way. Local retail discussion is dominated by “tariff, no future, 90% US exposure” framing, while data sites show negative trailing P/E and a tiny dividend. That screen makes it unownable for income investors and momentum investors at the same time. The setup only works for investors willing to underwrite forward cycle earnings rather than stale trailing losses.

The key risk is that the tariff is not temporary pain but a structural reset. If US customers force Malaysian suppliers to absorb much of the duty, Lii Hen’s old margin structure may never return. A second risk is demand: higher mortgage rates and weak furniture replacement spending can keep volumes depressed even without more tariff escalation. A third risk is capital allocation. Net cash is valuable only if it is not slowly consumed by low-return capacity, poor pricing, or family-controlled inertia.

Still, the asymmetry is unusually clear. The current quote is below net cash per share and at one-third of tangible book, while the company still has scale, US customer relationships, Muar capacity, and a history of earning MYR 17-75 million in better conditions. This is not a compounder. It is a cyclical, tariff-stressed, asset-backed recovery option where one or two quarters of stabilization could matter more than a polished long-term story.

Bear case: MYR 0.22, down 29% from MYR 0.31. Tariffs remain punitive, Q1 and Q2 2026 stay loss-making, cash is viewed as trapped, and the stock trades at about 0.23x book.

Base case: MYR 0.62, up 100%. Revenue stabilizes around MYR 575-600 million, net profit recovers to MYR 25-30 million, and the market values the business at 6-7x forward earnings plus partial credit for net cash.

Bull case: MYR 1.15, up 271%. US tariff pressure moderates or is passed through, housing/furniture demand recovers, utilization rises, net profit returns toward MYR 50-60 million, and Lii Hen rerates to 8x earnings while still carrying a large net cash balance.
